title: The levels of elite cultural esteem once lauded on Bellow and Updike are gone forever. Prestige fiction is dead — and that isn’t entirely a bad thing
description: The levels of elite cultural esteem once lauded on Bellow and Updike are gone forever. Prestige fiction is dead — and that isn’t entirely a bad thing
hook: The death of prestige fiction isn't a tragedy—it's liberation from a tyranny of taste that was always about gatekeeping, not art.

title: I Was Never the Kind of Indian Girl That Indian Guys Liked
description:
Aftereffects by Kalpana Narayanan When I meet Arun’s parents for the first time that September, I bring flowers. On the drive down, he tells me not to worry: his parents will love me. I am an actual woman that he is dating. His mother will probably want to give me some of her jewelry, he […]

The post I Was Never the Kind of Indian Girl That Indian Guys Liked appeared first on
hook: She was invisible to the men she wanted, hypervisible to the ones pursuing her—a story about being read wrong by everyone.

title: The Once and Future Bathhouse
description:
Utilitarian public baths and pools once filled the world’s cities, but now new expensive spas are taking their place. Has even bathing become a luxury?

The post The Once and Future Bathhouse appeared first on Public Books.
hook: Public bathing was once democratic. Now even washing yourself is a luxury good—how did we lose this?

title: Low Culture Podcast: Suicide’s Debut Album
description:
In this month's pod, Luke Turner and John Doran explore the beauty and violence of this masterpiece from Alan Vega and Martin Rev

Many moons ago, tQ heard Henry Rollins describing the time that he and Ian MacKaye had spent the afternoon listening to the 1977 debut album from New York duo Suicide, and were so disturbed by it – especially by the song ‘Frank
hook: Suicide's debut album is a masterpiece of beautiful violence, and this podcast digs into why beauty and brutality had to collide.

title: ‘I Love Boosters’ Review: Boots Riley’s Crazy Fashion Heist Comedy Is Both Stoner Movie and Anti-Capitalist Protest Art
description: SXSW: Keke Palmer, Naomi Ackie, Taylour Paige, and Demi Moore are all deeply attuned to Riley's weird rhythms, even as his high-concept sci-fi ideas grow scattershot.
hook: Boots Riley made a stoner heist movie that's somehow also a rigorous critique of fast fashion—and Keke Palmer is attuned to every weird beat.
